content.content:
1	---
2	name: gamut-director
3	description: Use when the user wants to produce a short generative video — a 10-30 second commercial, brand spot, montage, or trailer — entirely from a written brief, using only the `gamut` CLI + Fal / ElevenLabs AI backends + the web for reference. Triggers on "make a commercial", "generate a video ad", "produce a spot", "direct a video from a brief", "end-to-end generative video".
4	---
5	
6	# gamut-director — end-to-end generative video
7	
8	You are the director. Take a brief (or invent one), produce a finished MP4.
9	Every visible frame and every audible sample is AI-generated. No stock
10	footage, no hand-edited timeline, no manual asset wrangling.
11	
12	## What gamut actually is
13	
14	Gamut is a motion-graphics renderer. **Blitz** handles HTML layout,
15	**Stylo** (Servo's parallel CSS engine) computes styles and drives the
16	animation clock, **Parley** shapes text, **Animato** powers timeline
17	math, **Vello** rasterizes through wgpu, **rsmpeg** encodes h264/h265.
18	The render loop walks frame-by-frame, ticks the CSS animation engine via
19	`BaseDocument::resolve(now)` so every `@keyframes` rule and every
20	`transition` advances to the current scene time, paints to RGBA,
21	composites over a per-scene background video (and audio cues mixed
22	through rsmpeg), and writes an MP4 + sidecar WAV.
23	
24	The author surface is HTML. One file per scene (`scenes/01-title.html`,
25	`scenes/02-product.html`, …). One top-level `index.html` lists the
26	scenes and binds the audio. That is the entire authoring layer. No JSON
27	sidecars, no DSLs, no JS, no proprietary timeline format.
28	
29	**The hard rule:** this isn't a browser, but you write as if it is. If
30	something is standard CSS, assume it works — the exceptions are listed
31	explicitly below. Stylo and Blitz cover the bulk of the modern web
32	platform. Reach for the same idioms you'd use building a hand-crafted
33	landing page: `@keyframes`, `transition`, `clip-path`, `mix-blend-mode`,
34	flexbox, grid, gradients, `transform`, `cubic-bezier()`, web fonts via
35	`@font-face`. They render.
36	
37	**The anti-pattern this doc is written to prevent.** Every freshly-spun
38	agent writes the same thing on its first try: four scenes, all
39	`position: absolute; left: 80px; bottom: 80px; font: 900 88px Inter;`,
40	no `clip-path`, no `mix-blend-mode`, no animation beyond a single
41	`@keyframes fade-in`. That's the **AI-default lockup**. It looks like
42	every other AI commercial, and every senior creative director can spot
43	it in one frame. If your spot looks like every other AI ad, you skipped
44	the palette — go back and use it.
45	
46	## Tools you need
47	
48	- **`gamut` CLI** — the entire pipeline. Single binary at
49	  `packages/gamut/target/debug/gamut` from the repo root, or just `gamut`
50	  if it's on PATH.
51	- **The web** — research the subject of the commercial (palette, mood,
52	  reference shots) to inform your prompts. Use WebSearch/WebFetch.
53	- **Bash** — for parallel shot generation and one final ffmpeg mux step.
54	- **`FAL_KEY` + `ELEVENLABS_API_KEY`** — pre-exported in env. Don't
55	  print them. The CLI reads them.
56	
57	## The two production paths
58	
59	Two workflows, picked by whether the commercial is for a **real product**
60	(brand identity, recognizable silhouette — cars, watches, sneakers,
61	named landmarks) or a **brand-vibe** concept (fragrance, abstract
62	luxury, travel, energy drink — anything where AI hallucination is
63	acceptable).
64	
65	**Path B — reference-conditioned scene-still gen (the default for any
66	real product).** Collect 1-3 reference photos of the exact product.
67	For each scene, generate a scene-aware still that places the product
68	INTO that scene's lighting, angle, and perspective via
69	`gamut image scene-still` (Fal Seedream, ref-conditioned txt2img).
70	Then `img2vid` each scene-still. The product is generated fresh into
71	each shot's world — no cutout edges, no flat composites, no floating
72	subjects on black voids.
73	
74	```
75	web research → 1-3 high-quality reference photos (URLs)
76	            → for each scene:
77	                gamut image scene-still --refs <urls> --prompt "<scene>"
78	                gamut shot img2vid still-N.png "<motion>"
79	```
80	
81	**Path A — txt2vid only (brand-vibe only).**
82	Wan-T2V generates each shot from scratch. Cheap (~$0.10/shot), fast
83	(~30s/shot). Use for fragrance, travel, abstract concepts. Do NOT use
84	for real branded products — Wan hallucinates logos, badges, and text.
85	
86	**Legacy: bg-remove + composite (deprecated, kept for one narrow case).**
87	The old pipeline (`gamut image isolate` → `gamut shot still` →
88	`gamut image composite` → `img2vid`) still works, but it's only
89	recommended when you have a photographically-perfect reference and
90	just need to place it into a clean studio backdrop. For everything
91	else, scene-still gen avoids the cutout-seam failure mode.
92	
93	Cost per shot (Path B): $0.04 scene-still + $0.10 img2vid = $0.14.
94	
95	**The one hard rule for Path B.** Pick 1-3 high-quality reference photos
96	of the *same* product. All shots in the spot derive from those refs via
97	scene-still gen. Different scenes come from different *scene prompts*,
98	not from different reference photos of different cars. Refs lock the
99	product identity; scene prompts vary the world around it.
100	
101	## The pipeline (run in order)
102	
103	```
104	brief.md (9-line) → gamut brief check
105	            │
106	            ├─ write screenplay
107	            ├─ gamut screenplay parse → screenplay.json (sanity check)
108	            ├─ gamut velocity propose → velocity.json
109	            ├─ gamut storyboard plan  → storyboard.json
110	            ├─ gamut storyboard verify → must be 0 errors
111	            ├─ gamut continuity check  → must be 0 errors
112	            ├─ gamut transitions classify → transitions.json
113	            │
114	            ├─ gamut music gen → music.wav   (paid: ~$0.06)
115	            ├─ gamut velocity validate --against music.wav  (sanity)
116	            │
117	            ├─ for each scene:
118	            │   gamut shot txt2vid → shots/shot-N.mp4 (paid: ~$0.10 each)
119	            │
120	            ├─ write scene HTML overlays (the freeform palette section)
121	            ├─ assemble index.html manifest (or comp.json for advanced cases)
122	            └─ gamut render index.html -o commercial.mp4
123	```
124	
125	Canonical pipeline spec lives at `gamut pipelines show commercial`. The
126	workflow runner walks it cooperatively — `gamut workflow run commercial
127	--workdir .` reports the next stage based on which artifacts are on disk.
128	
129	## Gating spend with the reviewer
130	
131	After each stage produces an artifact — storyboard JSON, hero-panels
132	set, per-shot scene-still, per-shot i2v MP4, the final muxed cut —
133	invoke the `gamut-reviewer` skill with the stage name + artifact path
134	+ the brief. It returns a structured pass/warn/fail verdict and a
135	`spend_decision` of `proceed`, `iterate`, or `abort`. Honor it: only
136	move to the next paid step on `proceed`. On `iterate`, apply the
137	named remediation (re-roll, `fix-from-verify`, tighten the prompt)
138	and re-review. On `abort` (the same fail has recurred ≥ 3 times),
139	stop and report back rather than burning more budget. The reviewer
140	only reads — it never spends — so calling it between every stage is
141	free insurance against compounding errors.
142	
143	## Step 1 — pick a concept and write the 9-line brief
144	
145	A 10-15 second commercial works best. Good fits:
146	
147	- A consumer brand without specific logo demands (coffee, fragrance,
148	  EV concept, watch, travel)
149	- A travel destination
150	- A non-profit cause / public service spot
151	
152	Avoid:
153	
154	- Real people with dialogue (no lip-sync yet)
155	- Specific brand logos (Wan will hallucinate them poorly)
156	- Products that need close-ups of small written details
157	- Anything requiring text legibility in the generated footage
158	
159	### The 9-line ad creative brief
160	
161	Don't write prose. Write `brief.md` in the **9-line slot-filled
162	format**. One slot per line, in any order:
163	
164	| Slot       | What it captures                                       |
165	|------------|--------------------------------------------------------|
166	| `PRODUCT`  | What we're selling — one noun phrase                   |
167	| `AUDIENCE` | Who the spot is for — specific demographic, not "everyone" |
168	| `INSIGHT`  | What they currently believe/feel that the brand wants to shift |
169	| `PROMISE`  | What the brand says it will deliver                    |
170	| `PROOF`    | One concrete reason to believe the promise             |
171	| `TONE`     | Single-word aesthetic register (e.g. `cinematic`, `irreverent`, `brutalist`) |
172	| `MUSIC`    | Genre + energy curve (e.g. `ambient build → driving electronic peak`) |
173	| `CALL`     | What the viewer should do — CTA in 1-5 words           |
174	| `RUNTIME`  | Target duration in seconds (integer)                   |
175	
176	Worked example (`brief.md`):
177	
178	```markdown
179	PRODUCT: Allbirds Tree Runner sneakers
180	AUDIENCE: 28-40 urban professionals who walk more than they run
181	INSIGHT: "Sustainable" usually means uncomfortable or ugly
182	PROMISE: All-day comfort that happens to be made from trees
183	PROOF: Eucalyptus-fiber upper + sugarcane sole, machine washable
184	TONE: understated
185	MUSIC: acoustic minimal → warm indie-folk swell
186	CALL: Try them barefoot
187	RUNTIME: 15
188	```
189	
190	Validate before continuing:
191	
192	```bash
193	gamut brief check brief.md
194	```
195	
196	Long-form briefs are still acceptable as input. When a human hands you a
197	prose brief, distill it into the 9-line shape *before* moving to step 2.
198	
199	## Step 2 — write the screenplay
200	
201	Fountain format (`.fountain`). 4-6 scenes, mostly action paragraphs.
202	Match the screenplay's pacing to the commercial: short punchy action =
203	fast cuts; long flowing description = slower scenes.
204	
205	```fountain
206	Title: <product>
207	Author: gamut-director
208	
209	EXT. SAGUARO FIELD - DAY
210	
211	A giant cactus stands sentinel against the morning sky.
212	
213	CUT TO:
214	
215	EXT. SLOT CANYON - DAY
216	
217	Light cuts through the narrow walls of red stone.
218	
219	CUT TO:
220	
221	EXT. SEDONA VISTA - SUNSET
222	
223	Cliffs glow as the sun drops behind the ridge.
224	
225	CUT TO:
226	
227	EXT. DESERT ROAD - NIGHT
228	
229	Headlights cut a path through the silence.
230	
231	FADE OUT.
232	```
233	
234	Save to `script.fountain`.
235	
236	## Step 3 — run the agent-side pipeline
237	
238	All free, deterministic, and reversible:
239	
240	```bash
241	gamut screenplay parse script.fountain --pretty -o screenplay.json
242	gamut velocity propose script.fountain --pretty -o velocity.json
243	gamut storyboard plan script.fountain --velocity velocity.json --pretty -o storyboard.json
244	gamut storyboard verify storyboard.json
245	gamut continuity check storyboard.json
246	gamut transitions classify script.fountain --velocity velocity.json --pretty -o transitions.json
247	```
248	
249	Read each output. `velocity.json`'s `mean_bpm` tells you the music's
250	target tempo. `storyboard.json`'s `shots[].subject` tells you what each
251	shot is about. The continuity report flags 180° / motion / scale-jump
252	issues — if there are errors, reorder the screenplay or add a transition.
253	
254	## Step 3.25 — fill structured shot attributes (L-Storyboard)
255	
256	`Shot` carries an optional `attributes` block — seven typed slots that
257	replace freeform prose in the eventual model prompt:
258	
259	| Slot     | What it captures                                   |
260	|----------|----------------------------------------------------|
261	| subject  | what the shot is OF                                |
262	| action   | what's happening                                   |
263	| scene    | where it is (location + time of day + environment) |
264	| camera   | shot type + focal length + angle                   |
265	| lens     | optical character — DoF, anamorphic, fringe        |
266	| lighting | direction + quality of light                       |
267	| style    | aesthetic register, film stock, color grade        |
268	
269	```json
270	"attributes": {
271	  "subject": "a 1968 Porsche 911 GT3 in racing yellow",
272	  "action": "idles, engine off, parked at pit lane",
273	  "scene": "on wet asphalt as the sun crests the ridge",
274	  "camera": "WS 50mm, low angle, 3/4 front",
275	  "lens": "anamorphic, shallow DoF, slight chromatic fringe",
276	  "lighting": "backlit by rising sun, mist-diffused",
277	  "style": "cinematic, A24-flavored, restrained color"
278	}
279	```
280	
281	All seven required. If you don't know one, write the literal
282	`"unspecified"`. Reference fixture: `packages/gamut/tests/fixtures/l-storyboard-example.json`.
283	
284	## Step 3.26 — let an LLM fill the slots
285	
286	```bash
287	gamut director synthesize brief.md storyboard.json -o storyboard.dir.json --pretty
288	```
289	
290	Default Gemini 2.5 Pro via fal-ai/any-llm (~$0.02–$0.05/spot). Pass
291	`--model claude` for Opus 4.7 fallback. Read the output and patch the
292	two or three slots that drift.
293	
294	## Step 3.5 — generate the voiceover (optional)
295	
296	```bash
297	gamut dialogue tts "<your VO copy>" \
298	  --backend fal-kokoro \
299	  --voice af_nicole \
300	  --max-cost 0.05 \
301	  --out vo.wav \
302	  --pretty
303	```
304	
305	VO copy: fits the total duration (~2.5 words/sec is a comfortable read
306	pace), lands the brand or model name clearly with a 1-second pause for
307	emphasis, ends on a tagline or CTA.
308	
309	### Word-level captions (CapCut / Hormozi / minimal)
310	
311	```bash
312	gamut dialogue captions \
313	  --audio vo.wav --text "Fast cheap reliable big wins" \
314	  --backend fal-whisper-words --style hormozi \
315	  -o captions.json --pretty
316	
317	gamut captions overlay --in captions.json --style hormozi \
318	  --width 1080 --height 1920 -o caption.html
319	```
320	
321	The emitted `caption.html` is a normal scene HTML file — drop it into
322	your scene list as a sibling. CSS is `@keyframes`-only (no JS) so it
323	renders correctly through Blitz.
324	
325	## Step 4 — generate the music
326	
327	```bash
328	gamut music gen \
329	  --velocity velocity.json \
330	  --style "<2-3 line style description>" \
331	  --duration <total_secs> \
332	  --max-cost 0.10 \
333	  --out music.mp3 \
334	  --pretty
335	```
336	
337	Default `--backend elevenlabs` is the only commercially-safe option
338	(Merlin + Kobalt-licensed). `--backend fal-musicgen` is DEPRECATED —
339	pending litigation, not commercial-safe.
340	
341	Validate:
342	
343	```bash
344	gamut velocity validate velocity.json --against music.wav --tolerance 20 --pretty
345	```
346	
347	The validator now also writes a sibling `music.cuts.edl` when onsets are
348	detected. Use those onsets as snap targets for shot boundaries.
349	
350	## Step 5 — generate the shots
351	
352	### Path A: txt2vid (brand-vibe)
353	
354	One `gamut shot txt2vid` call per scene. Run them in parallel.
355	
356	```bash
357	gamut shot txt2vid \
358	  "<rich, specific prompt>" \
359	  --duration 5 --max-cost 0.20 \
360	  --out shots/shot-N-<name>.mp4 --pretty
361	```
362	
363	Prompt construction (in this order, comma-separated): subject, action,
364	setting, composition, atmosphere, tech. The standard negative prompt
365	(`"no text overlay, no watermark, no distortion, no extra limbs, low
366	quality, blurry"`) is appended automatically.
367	
368	### Path B: reference-conditioned scene-still + img2vid
369	
370	```bash
371	# B.1: hero panels lock the spot's look
372	gamut storyboard hero-panels storyboard.json -o shots/hero/ \
373	  --refs "https://.../ref-1.jpg" --layout 2x2 \
374	  --max-cost 0.10 --pretty
375	
376	# B.2: per-shot scene-still conditioned on the hero panel
377	gamut image scene-still \
378	  --refs "https://.../ref-1.jpg" \
379	  --hero-panel-ref "https://.../panel-2.png" \
380	  --prompt "<rich scene prompt>" \
381	  --image-size landscape_16_9 \
382	  --max-cost 0.05 --out shots/still-N.png --pretty
383	
384	# B.3: img2vid the scene-still
385	gamut shot img2vid shots/still-N.png "<short motion prompt>" \
386	  --duration 5 --max-cost 0.15 \
387	  --out shots/shot-N.mp4 --pretty
388	```
389	
390	Hero shots (1-2 per spot) run on `--backend nano-banana-pro` (~$0.24/img,
391	14 refs, 4K). Bulk shots run on default `seedream`.
392	
393	### Variant generation — roll N, pick the winner
394	
395	Every still / clip gen verb accepts `--variants N` (1-8, default 1).
396	
397	```bash
398	gamut image scene-still --refs https://… --prompt "…hero shot…" \
399	  --variants 3 --select max-vlm \
400	  --max-cost 0.05 --max-variants-cost 0.15 --pretty
401	```
402	
403	`--select` policies: `max-vlm` (default), `pairwise-tournament` (VISTA
404	bracket for identity-critical shots), `first`, `user`, `cheapest`.
405	
406	Use `pairwise-tournament` for hero shots and identity-critical SKUs;
407	`max-vlm` for everything else. Skip variants entirely for filler shots.
408	
409	## Step 6 — write the scene HTML overlays (FREEFORM)
410	
411	**This is the section that decides whether your spot looks AI-default or
412	art-directed.** Read it carefully.
413	
414	One `scenes/<id>.html` per scene composites *over* the generated video.
415	Blitz's CSS engine covers the bulk of the modern web platform: standard
416	CSS animations (`@keyframes`, `transition`, `cubic-bezier()`, `steps()`),
417	the full transform stack, clip-path, mix-blend-mode, all 16 blend modes,
418	gradients, web fonts, flexbox, grid, the works.
419	
420	Your training data is dense in standard HTML/CSS. Use it. Anything you'd
421	write on a hand-crafted brand site renders here.
422	
423	### Two structural rules
424	
425	1. **`html` and `body` must have `background: transparent`** so the
426	   generated video shows through where your HTML doesn't paint.
427	2. **The per-scene background video comes via the scene's `video_bg`
428	   field** (in `comp.json`) or via the top-level `<section
429	   data-scene-href="…">` reference (in `index.html`). Inline `<video>`
430	   inside a scene HTML file is not rendered today (see "What does not
431	   work" below). Top-level `<audio>` elements in `index.html` are bound
432	   to the comp's audio cue list — that's the canonical place for music
433	   + VO.
434	
435	### The supported palette — every one of these works today
436	
437	Pull from this whenever you're tempted to default to the bottom-left
438	lockup. One-line examples each; treat them as the toolkit.
439	
440	**Layout + position**
441	
442	```css
443	.frame { position: absolute; inset: 0; display: grid; grid-template-rows: 1fr auto; }
444	.lower-third { display: flex; gap: 1.2rem; align-items: flex-end; padding: 4rem; }
445	```
446	
447	`position: absolute / fixed / relative`, flexbox, grid, `inset`, `gap`,
448	`aspect-ratio`, `z-index`. `position: sticky` does not work (no scroll
449	in video).
450	
451	**Typography**
452	
453	```css
454	@import url("https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Bodoni+Moda:wght@400;900&display=swap");
455	@font-face { font-family: "Custom"; src: url("./fonts/custom.woff2") format("woff2"); }
456	.title { font: 900 240px/0.88 "Bodoni Moda", serif; letter-spacing: -0.04em; }
457	.subtitle { font: 200 18px "Inter", sans-serif; letter-spacing: 0.4em; text-transform: uppercase; }
458	```
459	
460	System fonts work. WOFF / WOFF2 via `@font-face`. Google Fonts via
461	`@import`. Variable font axes work where Stylo understands the
462	named-instance form. `line-height`, `letter-spacing`, `word-spacing`,
463	`text-transform`, `text-align`, `font-feature-settings`, `font-variant`
464	all work.
465	
466	**Color + gradients**
467	
468	```css
469	.scrim { background: linear-gradient(180deg, transparent 0%, #000a 60%, #000 100%); }
470	.aurora { background: conic-gradient(from 90deg at 30% 70%, #f06, #f60, #06f, #f06); }
471	.spot   { background: radial-gradient(ellipse at 30% 30%, #fffa 0%, transparent 60%); }
472	```
473	
474	Linear, radial, and conic gradients. Hex / rgb / rgba / hsl /
475	`color-mix()`. `currentColor` and CSS variables.
476	
477	**Transforms**
478	
479	```css
480	.tilt { transform: rotate(-3deg) scale(1.08) translateX(40px); transform-origin: top left; }
481	.skew { transform: skewX(-8deg); }
482	```
483	
484	`translate`, `scale`, `rotate`, `skew`, `matrix()`, `transform-origin`,
485	3D transforms (`rotateX/Y/Z`, `perspective`).
486	
487	**Borders, shadows, radius**
488	
489	```css
490	.card { border: 2px solid #fff; border-radius: 24px; box-shadow: 0 24px 80px #000c, inset 0 0 0 1px #fff2; }
491	```
492	
493	Multi-stop borders, `border-radius` (each corner independently),
494	`box-shadow` (multiple, including `inset`).
495	
496	**clip-path** — *use this*
497	
498	```css
499	.window { clip-path: circle(40% at 50% 50%); }
500	.notch  { clip-path: polygon(0 0, 100% 0, 100% 100%, 50% 70%, 0 100%); }
501	.tag    { clip-path: polygon(0 0, calc(100% - 24px) 0, 100% 50%, calc(100% - 24px) 100%, 0 100%); }
502	```
503	
504	`circle()`, `polygon()`, and the box-keyword forms (`margin-box`,
505	`border-box`, `padding-box`, `content-box`) render today.
506	`inset()`, `ellipse()`, `path()`, and `url(#mask)` are silently dropped
507	— pick a polygon or circle approximation if you need a rect-with-radius
508	window.
509	
510	**mix-blend-mode** — *use this*
511	
512	```css
513	.title-diff   { color: #fff; mix-blend-mode: difference; }
514	.title-screen { mix-blend-mode: screen; }
515	.title-mult   { mix-blend-mode: multiply; }
516	```
517	
518	All 16 CSS blend modes render through Vello's `peniko::Mix` —
519	`multiply, screen, overlay, darken, lighten, color-dodge, color-burn,
520	hard-light, soft-light, difference, exclusion, hue, saturation, color,
521	luminosity, normal`. `difference` over a generated shot is the canonical
522	"type carves through video" idiom and you should use it at least once
523	per spot.
524	
525	**filter** — *use this*
526	
527	```css
528	.soft     { filter: blur(8px); }
529	.poster   { filter: contrast(1.6) saturate(1.2); }
530	.mono     { filter: grayscale(1); }
531	.shifted  { filter: hue-rotate(180deg); }
532	.lifted   { filter: drop-shadow(0 12px 24px rgba(0,0,0,0.6)); }
533	.chained  { filter: blur(2px) saturate(1.3) brightness(1.1); }
534	```
535	
536	`blur(Npx)`, `saturate(N)`, `brightness(N)`, `contrast(N)`,
537	`grayscale(N)`, `hue-rotate(Ndeg)`, `invert(N)`, `sepia(N)`, `opacity(N)`,
538	and `drop-shadow(X Y B color)` all render. Chains apply left-to-right
539	per CSS spec. Implemented as a render-to-image fallback in
540	`vendor/blitz-paint/src/render/filter.rs` — the element is painted into
541	a sidecar Vello scene, the resulting RGBA buffer is filtered via
542	`image::imageops::blur` for spatial blur + standard `feColorMatrix`
543	matrices for color filters, then composited back into the parent scene
544	as an image brush. The fallback expands the painted region by 3·sigma
545	for blur and by the offset + 3·sigma for drop-shadow, so soft halos
546	don't get clipped.
547	
548	`backdrop-filter` does *not* render today (Stylo doesn't expose the
549	property under the Servo feature flag blitz-dom uses). Use a translucent
550	solid scrim or gradient scrim as a workaround.
551	
552	**Standard CSS animations** — *use this*
553	
554	```css
555	@keyframes title-in {
556	  0%   { transform: translateY(40px); opacity: 0; }
557	  60%  { transform: translateY(-4px); opacity: 1; }
558	  100% { transform: translateY(0);    opacity: 1; }
559	}
560	.title { animation: title-in 0.9s var(--ease-out-back) both; }
561	
562	.subtitle { transition: opacity 0.4s ease-out 0.3s; opacity: 0; }
563	.scene.live .subtitle { opacity: 1; }
564	```
565	
566	Stylo drives `@keyframes` and `transition` advance per-frame from the
567	scene clock — `render_offline.rs` resolves the document at every frame's
568	local time, so animations actually play in offline render. `linear`,
569	`ease`, `ease-in`, `ease-out`, `ease-in-out`, `cubic-bezier(…)`,
570	`steps(N [, jump-…])` all render. `animation-fill-mode`,
571	`animation-delay`, `animation-iteration-count`, `animation-direction`
572	work. Stagger by `:nth-child` + `animation-delay` math.
573	
574	Springs / bounces / elastic / wiggle aren't representable as
575	cubic-bezier. For those, lay out the animation as a multi-stop
576	`@keyframes` timeline by hand — Stylo interpolates per-stop.
577	
578	**Extended easing — paste from `eases.css`**
579	
580	A sibling file ships the standard easings.net curves as CSS custom
581	properties — `var(--ease-out-back)`, `var(--ease-out-expo)`,
582	`var(--ease-out-quint)`, etc., 24 named eases total. Copy the `:root`
583	block from `vendor/workbooks/skills/gamut-director/eases.css` into each
584	scene's `<style>` (or `@import` it). Two scenes in every spot should
585	reach into the extended table — `ease-out` is fine but identical use of
586	plain `ease` across every cut is the AI-default tell.
587	
588	```css
589	:root {
590	  --ease-out-back:  cubic-bezier(0.34, 1.56, 0.64, 1);
591	  --ease-out-quint: cubic-bezier(0.22, 1,    0.36, 1);
592	  --ease-out-expo:  cubic-bezier(0.16, 1,    0.3,  1);
593	  /* …see eases.css for the full set */
594	}
595	
596	.title  { animation: enter 0.9s var(--ease-out-back) both; }
597	.kicker { animation: enter 1.4s var(--ease-out-quint) 0.2s both; }
598	```
599	
600	**`<img>` element**
601	
602	```html
603	<img src="./logo.svg" style="width: 120px; opacity: 0.9;">
604	<img src="./moodboard-1.png" style="position: absolute; inset: 0; object-fit: cover;">
605	```
606	
607	Raster images (PNG / JPG / WebP) and SVG paint natively. Decoded via the
608	image crate's codec features in Blitz. `object-fit`, `object-position`
609	work.
610	
611	### Variation across cuts — a hard rule
612	
613	Within a single spot, **no two adjacent scenes may share the same
614	typographic treatment**. Same typeface across the spot is fine — that's
615	the through-line. Same size *and* position *and* motion is the
616	"AI-default" tell that flattens the work.
617	
618	The good failure mode: "scene 1 uses Bodoni 240px center, scene 2 uses
619	Inter 22px upper-right, scene 3 is type-free, scene 4 uses JetBrains
620	Mono 18px corner tags." The bad failure mode: "scene 1 is Inter 88px
621	bottom-left, scene 2 is Inter 88px bottom-left, scene 3 is Inter 88px
622	bottom-left, scene 4 is Inter 88px bottom-left."
623	
624	### Self-check before declaring a scene done
625	
626	Ask yourself:
627	
628	1. **Did I reuse the previous scene's lockup?** Same typeface + same
629	   size + same position + same motion. If yes — redo this one.
630	2. **Did I reach for `clip-path` or `mix-blend-mode` anywhere in the
631	   spot?** Neither one is mandatory in every scene, but a four-scene
632	   spot that doesn't touch either is using maybe 30% of the palette.
633	3. **Did I use anything from the extended ease table?** At least two
634	   scenes should be on `var(--ease-*)` curves, not plain `ease`.
635	4. **Did I let any scene have zero type?** Editorial silence is a real
636	   move. Captioning every cut isn't a requirement.
637	5. **Are my animations distinct per scene?** Four scenes all running the
638	   same `@keyframes slide-in` is the AI-default in motion form.
639	
640	### Anti-pattern gallery — do NOT ship these
641	
642	```css
643	/* ANTI-PATTERN 1: the AI-default lockup. Four scenes of this is failure. */
644	.title {
645	  position: absolute; left: 80px; bottom: 80px;
646	  font: 900 88px Inter, sans-serif;
647	  color: white;
648	  animation: fade-in 0.6s ease both;
649	}
650	```
651	
652	```css
653	/* ANTI-PATTERN 2: same @keyframes recycled across every scene */
654	@keyframes enter { from { opacity: 0; transform: translateY(20px); } to { opacity: 1; transform: none; } }
655	.scene-1-title { animation: enter 0.6s ease both; }
656	.scene-2-title { animation: enter 0.6s ease both; }
657	.scene-3-title { animation: enter 0.6s ease both; }
658	.scene-4-title { animation: enter 0.6s ease both; }
659	```
660	
661	```css
662	/* ANTI-PATTERN 3: everything centered, no negative-space awareness */
663	.scene { display: grid; place-items: center; }
664	.title { text-align: center; font-size: 120px; }
665	/* Repeated unmodified across all four scenes. */
666	```
667	
668	```css
669	/* ANTI-PATTERN 4: plain `ease` everywhere, no extended curves used */
670	.a { animation: in 0.5s ease both; }
671	.b { animation: in 0.5s ease-in-out both; }
672	.c { animation: in 0.5s ease both; }
673	.d { animation: in 0.5s ease both; }
674	```
675	
676	### Edge insets, contrast, reading rate — verify with the tools
677	
678	- **Title-safe** is the center 80% (10% inset off each edge). Type
679	  across title-safe is a deliberate choice (brutalist, display-driven).
680	  Type across action-safe (center 90%, 5% inset) is a mistake on most
681	  platforms. `gamut::aspect::safe_areas(w, h)` returns the right
682	  rectangles per aspect.
683	- **Read the shot first.** `gamut image negative-space <png>` returns
684	  the eye-friendly grid cells with suggested text color + scrim opacity.
685	  Position type in the top-ranked zone unless you have a reason to
686	  fight the shot.
687	- **Contrast.** `gamut image contrast <png> --region X,Y,W,H
688	  --text-color #...` reports WCAG ratio + suggests a scrim if below
689	  threshold. WCAG AA is 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for 18pt+ or 14pt
690	  bold. Below AA is a deliberate choice (luxury whisper, brutalist
691	  clash), not an accident.
692	- **Reading rate** is ~2.5 chars/sec. Kinetic word-by-word reveals live
693	  in the 0.25–0.4s range; faster than 0.15s reads as a flicker.
694	- **Existing text in the shot.** `gamut image ocr <png>` (stubbed
695	  pending Fal got-ocr) detects baked-in text so you don't stack overlays
696	  on plate numbers or storefront signage.

929	### Example 6 — Typographic mask: clip-path carves a word-shape into the video
930	
931	```html
932	<!doctype html>
933	<html><head><style>
934	  html, body { margin: 0; background: transparent; height: 100%; }
935	  .stage { position: absolute; inset: 0; display: grid; place-items: center; }
936	  /* Hex polygon that reads as a viewing port through the scene. */
937	  .port {
938	    width: 70vw; aspect-ratio: 16 / 7;
939	    background: #000;
940	    clip-path: polygon(8% 0, 92% 0, 100% 50%, 92% 100%, 8% 100%, 0% 50%);
941	    mix-blend-mode: lighten;
942	  }
943	  .legend {
944	    position: absolute; left: 8vw; bottom: 8vh;
945	    font: 800 22px "Helvetica Neue", sans-serif;
946	    color: #fff;
947	    letter-spacing: 0.18em; text-transform: uppercase;
948	  }
949	  @keyframes iris {
950	    from { clip-path: polygon(50% 50%, 50% 50%, 50% 50%, 50% 50%, 50% 50%, 50% 50%); }
951	    to   { clip-path: polygon(8% 0,    92% 0,    100% 50%, 92% 100%, 8% 100%, 0% 50%); }
952	  }

958	</body></html>
959	```

962	moving footage; `mix-blend-mode: lighten` keeps the brightest pixels

964	collapsed point to its full hexagonal aperture — an iris reveal.

1052	  .cta {
1053	    align-self: end; justify-self: end;
1054	    border: 1.5px solid #fff;
1055	    padding: 0.7em 1.4em;
1056	    font-size: 14px; letter-spacing: 0.22em; text-transform: uppercase;
1057	    border-radius: 999px;
1058	  }
1059	  @keyframes rise {
1060	    from { transform: translateY(8vh); opacity: 0; }
1061	    to   { transform: translateY(0);   opacity: 1; }
1062	  }
1063	  .pour { animation: rise 1.2s var(--ease-out-quint) 0.2s both; }
1064	</style></head>
1065	<body>
1066	  <div class="pour">
1067	    <div class="lockup">
1068	      <h2 class="name">Allbirds Tree Runner</h2>
1069	      <div class="strap">Made from trees — Walk on it</div>
1070	    </div>
1071	    <div class="cta">Try them barefoot</div>
1072	  </div>
1073	</body></html>
1074	```
1075	
1076	Design intent: classic broadcast lower-third, but with the extended
1077	`out-quint` curve so the rise feels weighted rather than rubbery. The
1078	CTA pill on the right balances the type on the left.
1079	
1080	### Example 10 — Multi-element kinetic stack with staggered eases
1081	
1082	```html
1083	<!doctype html>
1084	<html><head><style>
1085	  :root {
1086	    --ease-out-back:   cubic-bezier(0.34, 1.56, 0.64, 1);
1087	    --ease-out-quint:  cubic-bezier(0.22, 1,    0.36, 1);
1088	    --ease-out-circ:   cubic-bezier(0,    0.55, 0.45, 1);
1089	  }
1090	  html, body { margin: 0; background: transparent; height: 100%; font-family: "Inter", sans-serif; color: #fff; }
1091	  .stack {
1092	    position: absolute; left: 6vw; top: 50%;
1093	    transform: translateY(-50%);
1094	    display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 0.6em;
1095	    max-width: 56%;
1096	  }
1097	  .stack > * { opacity: 0; transform: translateX(-40px); }
1098	  .stack .eyebrow { font-size: 14px; letter-spacing: 0.3em; text-transform: uppercase; color: #ffd400; font-weight: 600; }
1099	  .stack .head    { font-size: 7vw; line-height: 1; font-weight: 900; letter-spacing: -0.03em; }
1100	  .stack .sub     { font-size: 18px; max-width: 36ch; color: #ffffffd0; line-height: 1.4; }
1101	  @keyframes slide-in {
1102	    to { opacity: 1; transform: translateX(0); }
1103	  }
1104	  .stack > :nth-child(1) { animation: slide-in 0.6s var(--ease-out-back)  0.15s forwards; }
1105	  .stack > :nth-child(2) { animation: slide-in 0.9s var(--ease-out-quint) 0.35s forwards; }
1106	  .stack > :nth-child(3) { animation: slide-in 1.1s var(--ease-out-circ)  0.65s forwards; }
1107	</style></head>
1108	<body>
1109	  <div class="stack">
1110	    <div class="eyebrow">FIELD TEST · DAY 12</div>
1111	    <h1 class="head">Built for the long walk home.</h1>
1112	    <p class="sub">Eucalyptus-fiber upper. Sugarcane sole. Machine washable. The kind of comfort you forget about — until you take them off.</p>
1113	  </div>
1114	</body></html>
1115	```
1116	
1117	Design intent: three vertically-stacked elements, each animating in on
1118	a different ease curve from the extended table. Staggered by
1119	`animation-delay`. The contrast between the back / quint / circ feel is
1120	the design — three siblings, three personalities.
1121	
1122	### Example 11 — Difference-mode title over a generated shot
1123	
1124	```html
1125	<!doctype html>
1126	<html><head><style>
1127	  html, body { margin: 0; background: transparent; height: 100%; font-family: "Helvetica Neue", sans-serif; }
1128	  .stage { position: absolute; inset: 0; display: grid; place-items: end center; padding: 6vh 0; }
1129	  .knockout {
1130	    font-weight: 900;
1131	    font-size: 16vw;
1132	    line-height: 0.92;
1133	    color: #fff;
1134	    mix-blend-mode: difference;
1135	    letter-spacing: -0.035em;
1136	    text-align: center;
1137	    margin: 0;
1138	  }
1139	  @keyframes settle {
1140	    0%   { letter-spacing: 0.2em;   opacity: 0; }
1141	    100% { letter-spacing: -0.035em; opacity: 1; }
1142	  }
1143	  .knockout { animation: settle 1.3s cubic-bezier(0.16, 1, 0.3, 1) both; }
1144	</style></head>
1145	<body>
1146	  <div class="stage">
1147	    <h1 class="knockout">EVERYWHERE.<br>NOWHERE.</h1>
1148	  </div>
1149	</body></html>
1150	```
1151	
1152	Design intent: the canonical "white type that carves through video".
1153	`mix-blend-mode: difference` makes the type readable over any underlying
1154	footage by inverting whatever's behind it. The kerning animation from
1155	wide to tight is the entry move.
1156	
1157	## What does NOT work
1158	
1159	The agent should never reach for these — they're silent no-ops at time
1160	of writing. If you write them, expect nothing to render.
1161	
1162	- **`background-clip: text`** — wb-wudl tracks this. Workaround: render
1163	  the gradient as a background on the element (the type itself stays
1164	  solid color) or use SVG `<text>` with a gradient fill.
1165	- **`text-shadow` (any variant)** — wb-o7s0 tracks this. Workaround:
1166	  stack two translated copies of the text with reduced opacity and a
1167	  bit of `transform: translate(1px,1px)` per layer to fake a glow.
1168	- **`backdrop-filter`** — Stylo doesn't expose the property under the
1169	  Servo feature flag blitz-dom uses (wb-3v87 tracks). The paint
1170	  pipeline can do it; the value isn't reachable. Use a translucent
1171	  solid scrim or a gradient scrim as a workaround for now.
1172	- **`clip-path: inset() / ellipse() / path() / xywh() / rect() /
1173	  url(#mask)`** — only `circle()`, `polygon()`, and box keywords ship.
1174	  Use a polygon for inset-with-radius shapes; use `border-radius` plus
1175	  `overflow: hidden` for plain rounded rectangles.
1176	- **`mask-image` / `mask`** — not wired through Stylo's style
1177	  computation. Use `clip-path: polygon(…)` as the closest substitute.
1178	- **JavaScript** — `<script>` tags are silently ignored. All animation
1179	  goes through CSS. There is no canvas, no JS-driven render hook, no
1180	  user event loop. Don't write JS.
1181	- **`<video>` element inside scene HTML** — not painted today. The
1182	  per-scene background video comes from the scene's `video_bg` field
1183	  (in `comp.json`) or via `<section data-scene-href="…">` in
1184	  `index.html`. Inline `<video>` is on the roadmap (wb-9h9u); once it
1185	  lands you'll place `<video src="shot.mp4" autoplay muted>` inline in
1186	  the scene HTML. **Today, use `video_bg` / `data-scene-href`.**
1187	- **`<audio>` element inside scene HTML** — same. Audio goes in
1188	  `comp.json`'s `audio_cues: [...]` array or as `<audio>` at the
1189	  top-level of `index.html`. Inline `<audio>` inside a scene is on the
1190	  roadmap (wb-ga6s).
1191	- **`position: sticky`, `:hover`, `:focus`, scroll-driven anything** —
1192	  there is no scroll, no hover, no focus in offline video render.
1193	  Selectors that depend on user interaction don't fire.
1194	- **`<iframe>`** — not supported.
1195	
1196	## Step 7 — assemble the multi-scene manifest
1197	
1198	The canonical authoring path is a top-level `index.html` that lists the
1199	scenes and their audio cues. The renderer parses it via
1200	`packages/gamut/src/compose/mod.rs` and resolves relative paths against
1201	the manifest's parent directory.
1202	
1203	```html
1204	<!doctype html>
1205	<html><head>
1206	  <title>Tree Runner Spot</title>
1207	  <meta name="resolution" content="1280x720">
1208	  <meta name="fps" content="30">
1209	  <meta name="duration" content="15s">
1210	</head><body>
1211	  <section data-scene-href="scenes/01-title.html"   data-duration="3s"></section>
1212	  <section data-scene-href="scenes/02-product.html" data-duration="6s"
1213	           data-transition-in="crossfade" data-transition-duration="0.5s"></section>
1214	  <section data-scene-href="scenes/03-detail.html"  data-duration="3s"></section>
1215	  <section data-scene-href="scenes/04-cta.html"     data-duration="3s"
1216	           data-transition-in="crossfade" data-transition-duration="0.4s"></section>
1217	
1218	  <audio src="music/track.wav" data-spans="all" data-volume="0.8" data-fade-in="0.4s" data-fade-out="1s"></audio>
1219	  <audio src="vo/line.wav"     data-start="6s" data-duration="3s" data-fade-in="0.2s"></audio>
1220	</body></html>
1221	```
1222	
1223	Required `<meta>`: `resolution` (`WxH`) and `fps`. `duration` is
1224	optional — if omitted, the composition duration is the sum of scene
1225	durations.
1226	
1227	**`<section>` attributes:**
1228	
1229	| Attribute                  | Required | What it means                              |
1230	|----------------------------|----------|--------------------------------------------|
1231	| `data-scene-href`          | yes      | Relative path to the scene HTML file       |
1232	| `data-duration`            | yes      | `3s`, `1500ms`, or plain integer seconds   |
1233	| `data-transition-in`       | no       | `cut` (default), `crossfade`, `fade`, `shader:<name>` |
1234	| `data-transition-duration` | no       | Duration of the transition; default `0.5s` |
1235	
1236	**`<audio>` attributes:**
1237	
1238	| Attribute       | What it means                                                  |
1239	|-----------------|----------------------------------------------------------------|
1240	| `src`           | Asset path (relative). REQUIRED.                                |
1241	| `data-spans`    | `all` — bind to the full composition duration                  |
1242	| `data-start`    | Start offset, default `0s`                                     |
1243	| `data-duration` | Explicit duration; default 0 (use until end)                   |
1244	| `data-fade-in`  | Fade-in duration                                                |
1245	| `data-fade-out` | Fade-out duration                                              |
1246	| `data-volume`   | Float 0..1, default `1.0`                                      |
1247	
1248	Per-scene background video is currently still wired via `comp.json`'s
1249	`video_bg` field — the `<section>` manifest reads that path from the
1250	scene's parent `comp.json` when present, otherwise the scene renders
1251	with transparent background only. Once wb-9h9u lands the scene HTML
1252	itself will carry the `<video>` tag inline.
1253	
1254	Render with:
1255	
1256	```bash
1257	gamut render index.html -o commercial.mp4
1258	```
1259	
1260	The CLI auto-detects `.html` vs `.json` and routes to `load_index_html`
1261	or `Composition::from_json_path` accordingly.
1262	
1263	## Step 7.5 — the comp.json escape hatch
1264	
1265	For tests, advanced motion control, and per-scene `video_bg` wiring
1266	you'd rather not stuff into the HTML manifest, `comp.json` is the
1267	lower-level format. The same `gamut render comp.json -o out.mp4`
1268	command works.
1269	
1270	```json
1271	{
1272	  "width": 1280,
1273	  "height": 720,
1274	  "fps": 30,
1275	  "duration_frames": 360,
1276	  "scenes": [
1277	    {
1278	      "html_path": "scenes/01-title.html",
1279	      "video_bg": "shots/shot-1-saguaro.mp4",
1280	      "start_frame": 0,
1281	      "duration_frames": 90
1282	    }
1283	  ],
1284	  "audio_cues": [
1285	    {
1286	      "id": "music",
1287	      "asset_path": "music.wav",
1288	      "start_frame": 0,
1289	      "duration_frames": 360,
1290	      "volume": 0.7,
1291	      "pan": 0.0,
1292	      "fade_in_frames": 6,
1293	      "fade_out_frames": 24,
1294	      "duck_targets": [],
1295	      "duck_db": 0.0
1296	    }
1297	  ]
1298	}
1299	```
1300	
1301	Required fields per scene: `html_path`, `start_frame`, `duration_frames`.
1302	Optional: `video_bg`, `transition_in`. Frame math: at 30fps,
1303	`duration_frames = 30 * secs`. Scene durations should sum to the total —
1304	don't leave gaps.
1305	
1306	Reference: `packages/gamut/examples/arizona/comp.json` is a working
1307	multi-scene composition.
1308	
1309	> Historical note: a `motion: [...]` array used to live on each scene
1310	> as a 7-property enum (`x, y, scale, rotate, opacity, width, height`).
1311	> That layer is **retired** — animation lives in standard CSS now via
1312	> Stylo's `@keyframes` + `transition` engine. Don't reach for it.
1313	
1314	## Step 8 — render and (optionally) re-mux
1315	
1316	```bash
1317	gamut render index.html -o commercial.mp4
1318	```
1319	
1320	When the composition has audio cues, render emits a sidecar `.wav`
1321	alongside the video. If you need a finer-grained audio path (different
1322	codec, additional ducking, ffmpeg-side normalization), re-mux:
1323	
1324	```bash
1325	ffmpeg -y -i commercial.mp4 -i commercial.wav \
1326	  -c:v copy -c:a aac -b:a 192k -shortest \
1327	  commercial.muxed.mp4
1328	```
1329	
1330	## Step 8.5 — provenance signing (C2PA)
1331	
1332	EU AI Act Article 50 enforcement begins **August 2026**. Sign at render time:
1333	
1334	```bash
1335	gamut render index.html -o commercial.mp4 \
1336	  --sign-c2pa --title "Brand spot v3" --author "Studio name"
1337	```
1338	
1339	Or retroactively:
1340	
1341	```bash
1342	gamut c2pa sign commercial.mp4 -o commercial.signed.mp4 \
1343	  --comp index.html --title "Brand spot v3" --author "Studio name"
1344	gamut c2pa verify commercial.signed.mp4
1345	```
1346	
1347	The bundled test cert chains to a non-trusted root — fine for
1348	development, not for delivery. For production, BYO cert chain that
1349	traces to a C2PA-trusted root via `--signing-cert` + `--signing-key`.
1350	
1351	## Step 8.6 — optional premium finish (Topaz Astra 2)
1352	
1353	Manual post-step. Drag `commercial.mp4` into Topaz Astra 2, pick the
1354	`Proteus` preset, export to `commercial.astra.mp4`, then re-sign:
1355	
1356	```bash
1357	gamut c2pa sign commercial.astra.mp4 --comp index.html
1358	```
1359	
1360	Astra's re-encode invalidates the original C2PA hash, so re-signing
1361	is mandatory.
1362	
1363	## Verifying the result
1364	
1365	Extract a frame from the rendered video and spot-check:
1366	
1367	```bash
1368	ffmpeg -ss 1.5 -i commercial.mp4 -vframes 1 frame.jpg
1369	```
1370	
1371	Confirm: the expected scene's subject is visible, the HTML overlay is
1372	readable, the frame matches the AI-generated content (not a fallback).
1373	Pull a frame at each scene boundary (0.5s, mid-scene, last 0.5s) to
1374	verify the CSS animations actually played.
1375	
1376	## Budget guidelines
1377	
1378	Default ceiling: **$1.00 total** for a 12-second commercial.
1379	
1380	- Music (12s × $0.005/s): ~$0.06
1381	- Shots (4-6 × $0.10): $0.40-0.60
1382	- Re-render: free (cache hits)
1383	- Re-generate one shot: $0.10
1384	- Final mux: free
1385	
1386	Always pass `--max-cost <N>` on every billed call. The CLI refuses
1387	requests where estimate exceeds the budget.
1388	
1389	## File layout convention
1390	
1391	```
1392	/tmp/gamut-commercial/
1393	  brief.md
1394	  script.fountain
1395	  screenplay.json
1396	  velocity.json
1397	  storyboard.json
1398	  transitions.json
1399	  music/
1400	    track.wav
1401	  vo/
1402	    line.wav            (optional)
1403	  eases.css             (the :root block from skills/gamut-director/eases.css)
1404	  scenes/
1405	    01-title.html
1406	    02-canyon.html
1407	    03-vista.html
1408	    04-road.html
1409	  shots/
1410	    still-1.png         (Path B: scene-still per scene)
1411	    still-2.png
1412	    shot-1-saguaro.mp4
1413	    shot-2-canyon.mp4
1414	    shot-3-sedona.mp4
1415	    shot-4-road.mp4
1416	  index.html            (the multi-scene manifest — feed this to gamut render)
1417	  commercial.mp4        (the deliverable)
1418	  commercial.wav        (sidecar audio if you need to re-mux)
1419	```
1420	
1421	## Common pitfalls
1422	
1423	- **Stock-looking generated shots:** Prompts too generic. Add subject
1424	  specifics, composition, camera type. Not "a desert" — "a single
1425	  saguaro silhouetted against a flame-orange sunset, mountains on the
1426	  horizon, wide low-angle, cinematic".
1427	- **Black frames in render:** A `video_bg` path didn't resolve. Check
1428	  paths in `comp.json` (or your wiring) are relative to the manifest's
1429	  directory.
1430	- **Audio out of sync:** Music duration must equal total render
1431	  duration. Generate music to match (`--duration <total_secs>`) or use
1432	  `<audio data-spans="all">` to bind to the comp's duration explicitly.
1433	- **Continuity check fails:** Two adjacent shots cross the 180° line.
1434	  Reorder shots in the screenplay or add a `WHIP PAN TO:` /
1435	  `SMASH CUT TO:` between them.
1436	- **Wan output doesn't match prompt:** Re-roll with a different seed
1437	  (`--seed <N>`) or rewrite the prompt to be more specific.
1438	- **Animations don't play in the rendered MP4:** Verify the scene HTML
1439	  uses standard CSS `@keyframes` (not the retired `motion: [...]` JSON
1440	  format). Stylo runs the clock per-frame; bad `cubic-bezier()`
1441	  arguments outside `[0,1]` for x-axis silently fall back.
1442	- **Spot looks like every other AI ad:** You used the AI-default
1443	  lockup. Same Inter-88px-bottom-left across all four scenes, no
1444	  `clip-path`, no `mix-blend-mode`, every animation on plain `ease`.
1445	  Go back, vary the typography per scene, reach into the extended
1446	  ease table on at least two cuts, use `mix-blend-mode: difference`
1447	  on at least one title.
1448	- **`filter: blur(8px)` did nothing:** Filter support hasn't landed —
1449	  see "What does NOT work". Pre-blur the underlying shot in the image
1450	  gen prompt, or accept the overlay can't blur the backdrop.
1451	- **`background-clip: text` didn't apply a gradient to type:** Same —
1452	  not shipping yet. Render the gradient as the element's background
1453	  (solid type, gradient field) or move to an SVG `<text>` with
1454	  `<linearGradient>` fill.
1455	
1456	## When you're done
1457	
1458	Report:
1459	
1460	- Path to the final muxed MP4
1461	- Total billed spend
1462	- Which generation steps succeeded / required retry
1463	- Anything in the brief you couldn't honor (and why)
1464	
1465	Don't open the file for the user — they'll do that themselves.
1466	
1467	---
1468	
1469	*Last updated 2026-05-19 — closes bd issue **wb-ndw7** (child of epic
1470	wb-e8jh). Verify the "shipping today / coming soon" lists in this doc
1471	against the current state of `vendor/blitz-paint/src/render/` and
1472	`packages/gamut/src/compose/` on every release; this file describes the
1473	palette as of that date.*
1474

file.content:
---
name: gamut-director
description: Use when the user wants to produce a short generative video — a 10-30 second commercial, brand spot, montage, or trailer — entirely from a written brief, using only the `gamut` CLI + Fal / ElevenLabs AI backends + the web for reference. Triggers on "make a commercial", "generate a video ad", "produce a spot", "direct a video from a brief", "end-to-end generative video".
---

# gamut-director — end-to-end generative video

You are the director. Take a brief (or invent one), produce a finished MP4.
Every visible frame and every audible sample is AI-generated. No stock
footage, no hand-edited timeline, no manual asset wrangling.

## What gamut actually is

Gamut is a motion-graphics renderer. **Blitz** handles HTML layout,
**Stylo** (Servo's parallel CSS engine) computes styles and drives the
animation clock, **Parley** shapes text, **Animato** powers timeline
math, **Vello** rasterizes through wgpu, **rsmpeg** encodes h264/h265.
The render loop walks frame-by-frame, ticks the CSS animation engine via
`BaseDocument::resolve(now)` so every `@keyframes` rule and every
`transition` advances to the current scene time, paints to RGBA,
composites over a per-scene background video (and audio cues mixed
through rsmpeg), and writes an MP4 + sidecar WAV.

The author surface is HTML. One file per scene (`scenes/01-title.html`,
`scenes/02-product.html`, …). One top-level `index.html` lists the
scenes and binds the audio. That is the entire authoring layer. No JSON
sidecars, no DSLs, no JS, no proprietary timeline format.

**The hard rule:** this isn't a browser, but you write as if it is. If
something is standard CSS, assume it works — the exceptions are listed
explicitly below. Stylo and Blitz cover the bulk of the modern web
platform. Reach for the same idioms you'd use building a hand-crafted
landing page: `@keyframes`, `transition`, `clip-path`, `mix-blend-mode`,
flexbox, grid, gradients, `transform`, `cubic-bezier()`, web fonts via
`@font-face`. They render.

**The anti-pattern this doc is written to prevent.** Every freshly-spun
agent writes the same thing on its first try: four scenes, all
`position: absolute; left: 80px; bottom: 80px; font: 900 88px Inter;`,
no `clip-path`, no `mix-blend-mode`, no animation beyond a single
`@keyframes fade-in`. That's the **AI-default lockup**. It looks like
every other AI commercial, and every senior creative director can spot
it in one frame. If your spot looks like every other AI ad, you skipped
the palette — go back and use it.

## Tools you need

- **`gamut` CLI** — the entire pipeline. Single binary at
  `packages/gamut/target/debug/gamut` from the repo root, or just `gamut`
  if it's on PATH.
- **The web** — research the subject of the commercial (palette, mood,
  reference shots) to inform your prompts. Use WebSearch/WebFetch.
- **Bash** — for parallel shot generation and one final ffmpeg mux step.
- **`FAL_KEY` + `ELEVENLABS_API_KEY`** — pre-exported in env. Don't
  print them. The CLI reads them.

## The two production paths

Two workflows, picked by whether the commercial is for a **real product**
(brand identity, recognizable silhouette — cars, watches, sneakers,
named landmarks) or a **brand-vibe** concept (fragrance, abstract
luxury, travel, energy drink — anything where AI hallucination is
acceptable).

**Path B — reference-conditioned scene-still gen (the default for any
real product).** Collect 1-3 reference photos of the exact product.
For each scene, generate a scene-aware still that places the product
INTO that scene's lighting, angle, and perspective via
`gamut image scene-still` (Fal Seedream, ref-conditioned txt2img).
Then `img2vid` each scene-still. The product is generated fresh into
each shot's world — no cutout edges, no flat composites, no floating
subjects on black voids.

```
web research → 1-3 high-quality reference photos (URLs)
            → for each scene:
                gamut image scene-still --refs <urls> --prompt "<scene>"
                gamut shot img2vid still-N.png "<motion>"
```

**Path A — txt2vid only (brand-vibe only).**
Wan-T2V generates each shot from scratch. Cheap (~$0.10/shot), fast
(~30s/shot). Use for fragrance, travel, abstract concepts. Do NOT use
for real branded products — Wan hallucinates logos, badges, and text.

**Legacy: bg-remove + composite (deprecated, kept for one narrow case).**
The old pipeline (`gamut image isolate` → `gamut shot still` →
`gamut image composite` → `img2vid`) still works, but it's only
recommended when you have a photographically-perfect reference and
just need to place it into a clean studio backdrop. For everything
else, scene-still gen avoids the cutout-seam failure mode.

Cost per shot (Path B): $0.04 scene-still + $0.10 img2vid = $0.14.

**The one hard rule for Path B.** Pick 1-3 high-quality reference photos
of the *same* product. All shots in the spot derive from those refs via
scene-still gen. Different scenes come from different *scene prompts*,
not from different reference photos of different cars. Refs lock the
product identity; scene prompts vary the world around it.

## The pipeline (run in order)

```
brief.md (9-line) → gamut brief check
            │
            ├─ write screenplay
            ├─ gamut screenplay parse → screenplay.json (sanity check)
            ├─ gamut velocity propose → velocity.json
            ├─ gamut storyboard plan  → storyboard.json
            ├─ gamut storyboard verify → must be 0 errors
            ├─ gamut continuity check  → must be 0 errors
            ├─ gamut transitions classify → transitions.json
            │
            ├─ gamut music gen → music.wav   (paid: ~$0.06)
            ├─ gamut velocity validate --against music.wav  (sanity)
            │
            ├─ for each scene:
            │   gamut shot txt2vid → shots/shot-N.mp4 (paid: ~$0.10 each)
            │
            ├─ write scene HTML overlays (the freeform palette section)
            ├─ assemble index.html manifest (or comp.json for advanced cases)
            └─ gamut render index.html -o commercial.mp4
```

Canonical pipeline spec lives at `gamut pipelines show commercial`. The
workflow runner walks it cooperatively — `gamut workflow run commercial
--workdir .` reports the next stage based on which artifacts are on disk.

## Gating spend with the reviewer

After each stage produces an artifact — storyboard JSON, hero-panels
set, per-shot scene-still, per-shot i2v MP4, the final muxed cut —
invoke the `gamut-reviewer` skill with the stage name + artifact path
+ the brief. It returns a structured pass/warn/fail verdict and a
`spend_decision` of `proceed`, `iterate`, or `abort`. Honor it: only
move to the next paid step on `proceed`. On `iterate`, apply the
named remediation (re-roll, `fix-from-verify`, tighten the prompt)
and re-review. On `abort` (the same fail has recurred ≥ 3 times),
stop and report back rather than burning more budget. The reviewer
only reads — it never spends — so calling it between every stage is
free insurance against compounding errors.

## Step 1 — pick a concept and write the 9-line brief

A 10-15 second commercial works best. Good fits:

- A consumer brand without specific logo demands (coffee, fragrance,
  EV concept, watch, travel)
- A travel destination
- A non-profit cause / public service spot

Avoid:

- Real people with dialogue (no lip-sync yet)
- Specific brand logos (Wan will hallucinate them poorly)
- Products that need close-ups of small written details
- Anything requiring text legibility in the generated footage

### The 9-line ad creative brief

Don't write prose. Write `brief.md` in the **9-line slot-filled
format**. One slot per line, in any order:

| Slot       | What it captures                                       |
|------------|--------------------------------------------------------|
| `PRODUCT`  | What we're selling — one noun phrase                   |
| `AUDIENCE` | Who the spot is for — specific demographic, not "everyone" |
| `INSIGHT`  | What they currently believe/feel that the brand wants to shift |
| `PROMISE`  | What the brand says it will deliver                    |
| `PROOF`    | One concrete reason to believe the promise             |
| `TONE`     | Single-word aesthetic register (e.g. `cinematic`, `irreverent`, `brutalist`) |
| `MUSIC`    | Genre + energy curve (e.g. `ambient build → driving electronic peak`) |
| `CALL`     | What the viewer should do — CTA in 1-5 words           |
| `RUNTIME`  | Target duration in seconds (integer)                   |

Worked example (`brief.md`):

```markdown
PRODUCT: Allbirds Tree Runner sneakers
AUDIENCE: 28-40 urban professionals who walk more than they run
INSIGHT: "Sustainable" usually means uncomfortable or ugly
PROMISE: All-day comfort that happens to be made from trees
PROOF: Eucalyptus-fiber upper + sugarcane sole, machine washable
TONE: understated
MUSIC: acoustic minimal → warm indie-folk swell
CALL: Try them barefoot
RUNTIME: 15
```

Validate before continuing:

```bash
gamut brief check brief.md
```

Long-form briefs are still acceptable as input. When a human hands you a
prose brief, distill it into the 9-line shape *before* moving to step 2.

## Step 2 — write the screenplay

Fountain format (`.fountain`). 4-6 scenes, mostly action paragraphs.
Match the screenplay's pacing to the commercial: short punchy action =
fast cuts; long flowing description = slower scenes.

```fountain
Title: <product>
Author: gamut-director

EXT. SAGUARO FIELD - DAY

A giant cactus stands sentinel against the morning sky.

CUT TO:

EXT. SLOT CANYON - DAY

Light cuts through the narrow walls of red stone.

CUT TO:

EXT. SEDONA VISTA - SUNSET

Cliffs glow as the sun drops behind the ridge.

CUT TO:

EXT. DESERT ROAD - NIGHT

Headlights cut a path through the silence.

FADE OUT.
```

Save to `script.fountain`.

## Step 3 — run the agent-side pipeline

All free, deterministic, and reversible:

```bash
gamut screenplay parse script.fountain --pretty -o screenplay.json
gamut velocity propose script.fountain --pretty -o velocity.json
gamut storyboard plan script.fountain --velocity velocity.json --pretty -o storyboard.json
gamut storyboard verify storyboard.json
gamut continuity check storyboard.json
gamut transitions classify script.fountain --velocity velocity.json --pretty -o transitions.json
```

Read each output. `velocity.json`'s `mean_bpm` tells you the music's
target tempo. `storyboard.json`'s `shots[].subject` tells you what each
shot is about. The continuity report flags 180° / motion / scale-jump
issues — if there are errors, reorder the screenplay or add a transition.

## Step 3.25 — fill structured shot attributes (L-Storyboard)

`Shot` carries an optional `attributes` block — seven typed slots that
replace freeform prose in the eventual model prompt:

| Slot     | What it captures                                   |
|----------|----------------------------------------------------|
| subject  | what the shot is OF                                |
| action   | what's happening                                   |
| scene    | where it is (location + time of day + environment) |
| camera   | shot type + focal length + angle                   |
| lens     | optical character — DoF, anamorphic, fringe        |
| lighting | direction + quality of light                       |
| style    | aesthetic register, film stock, color grade        |

```json
"attributes": {
  "subject": "a 1968 Porsche 911 GT3 in racing yellow",
  "action": "idles, engine off, parked at pit lane",
  "scene": "on wet asphalt as the sun crests the ridge",
  "camera": "WS 50mm, low angle, 3/4 front",
  "lens": "anamorphic, shallow DoF, slight chromatic fringe",
  "lighting": "backlit by rising sun, mist-diffused",
  "style": "cinematic, A24-flavored, restrained color"
}
```

All seven required. If you don't know one, write the literal
`"unspecified"`. Reference fixture: `packages/gamut/tests/fixtures/l-storyboard-example.json`.

## Step 3.26 — let an LLM fill the slots

```bash
gamut director synthesize brief.md storyboard.json -o storyboard.dir.json --pretty
```

Default Gemini 2.5 Pro via fal-ai/any-llm (~$0.02–$0.05/spot). Pass
`--model claude` for Opus 4.7 fallback. Read the output and patch the
two or three slots that drift.

## Step 3.5 — generate the voiceover (optional)

```bash
gamut dialogue tts "<your VO copy>" \
  --backend fal-kokoro \
  --voice af_nicole \
  --max-cost 0.05 \
  --out vo.wav \
  --pretty
```

VO copy: fits the total duration (~2.5 words/sec is a comfortable read
pace), lands the brand or model name clearly with a 1-second pause for
emphasis, ends on a tagline or CTA.

### Word-level captions (CapCut / Hormozi / minimal)

```bash
gamut dialogue captions \
  --audio vo.wav --text "Fast cheap reliable big wins" \
  --backend fal-whisper-words --style hormozi \
  -o captions.json --pretty

gamut captions overlay --in captions.json --style hormozi \
  --width 1080 --height 1920 -o caption.html
```

The emitted `caption.html` is a normal scene HTML file — drop it into
your scene list as a sibling. CSS is `@keyframes`-only (no JS) so it
renders correctly through Blitz.

## Step 4 — generate the music

```bash
gamut music gen \
  --velocity velocity.json \
  --style "<2-3 line style description>" \
  --duration <total_secs> \
  --max-cost 0.10 \
  --out music.mp3 \
  --pretty
```

Default `--backend elevenlabs` is the only commercially-safe option
(Merlin + Kobalt-licensed). `--backend fal-musicgen` is DEPRECATED —
pending litigation, not commercial-safe.

Validate:

```bash
gamut velocity validate velocity.json --against music.wav --tolerance 20 --pretty
```

The validator now also writes a sibling `music.cuts.edl` when onsets are
detected. Use those onsets as snap targets for shot boundaries.

## Step 5 — generate the shots

### Path A: txt2vid (brand-vibe)

One `gamut shot txt2vid` call per scene. Run them in parallel.

```bash
gamut shot txt2vid \
  "<rich, specific prompt>" \
  --duration 5 --max-cost 0.20 \
  --out shots/shot-N-<name>.mp4 --pretty
```

Prompt construction (in this order, comma-separated): subject, action,
setting, composition, atmosphere, tech. The standard negative prompt
(`"no text overlay, no watermark, no distortion, no extra limbs, low
quality, blurry"`) is appended automatically.

### Path B: reference-conditioned scene-still + img2vid

```bash
# B.1: hero panels lock the spot's look
gamut storyboard hero-panels storyboard.json -o shots/hero/ \
  --refs "https://.../ref-1.jpg" --layout 2x2 \
  --max-cost 0.10 --pretty

# B.2: per-shot scene-still conditioned on the hero panel
gamut image scene-still \
  --refs "https://.../ref-1.jpg" \
  --hero-panel-ref "https://.../panel-2.png" \
  --prompt "<rich scene prompt>" \
  --image-size landscape_16_9 \
  --max-cost 0.05 --out shots/still-N.png --pretty

# B.3: img2vid the scene-still
gamut shot img2vid shots/still-N.png "<short motion prompt>" \
  --duration 5 --max-cost 0.15 \
  --out shots/shot-N.mp4 --pretty
```

Hero shots (1-2 per spot) run on `--backend nano-banana-pro` (~$0.24/img,
14 refs, 4K). Bulk shots run on default `seedream`.

### Variant generation — roll N, pick the winner

Every still / clip gen verb accepts `--variants N` (1-8, default 1).

```bash
gamut image scene-still --refs https://… --prompt "…hero shot…" \
  --variants 3 --select max-vlm \
  --max-cost 0.05 --max-variants-cost 0.15 --pretty
```

`--select` policies: `max-vlm` (default), `pairwise-tournament` (VISTA
bracket for identity-critical shots), `first`, `user`, `cheapest`.

Use `pairwise-tournament` for hero shots and identity-critical SKUs;
`max-vlm` for everything else. Skip variants entirely for filler shots.

## Step 6 — write the scene HTML overlays (FREEFORM)

**This is the section that decides whether your spot looks AI-default or
art-directed.** Read it carefully.

One `scenes/<id>.html` per scene composites *over* the generated video.
Blitz's CSS engine covers the bulk of the modern web platform: standard
CSS animations (`@keyframes`, `transition`, `cubic-bezier()`, `steps()`),
the full transform stack, clip-path, mix-blend-mode, all 16 blend modes,
gradients, web fonts, flexbox, grid, the works.

Your training data is dense in standard HTML/CSS. Use it. Anything you'd
write on a hand-crafted brand site renders here.

### Two structural rules

1. **`html` and `body` must have `background: transparent`** so the
   generated video shows through where your HTML doesn't paint.
2. **The per-scene background video comes via the scene's `video_bg`
   field** (in `comp.json`) or via the top-level `<section
   data-scene-href="…">` reference (in `index.html`). Inline `<video>`
   inside a scene HTML file is not rendered today (see "What does not
   work" below). Top-level `<audio>` elements in `index.html` are bound
   to the comp's audio cue list — that's the canonical place for music
   + VO.

### The supported palette — every one of these works today

Pull from this whenever you're tempted to default to the bottom-left
lockup. One-line examples each; treat them as the toolkit.

**Layout + position**

```css
.frame { position: absolute; inset: 0; display: grid; grid-template-rows: 1fr auto; }
.lower-third { display: flex; gap: 1.2rem; align-items: flex-end; padding: 4rem; }
```

`position: absolute / fixed / relative`, flexbox, grid, `inset`, `gap`,
`aspect-ratio`, `z-index`. `position: sticky` does not work (no scroll
in video).

**Typography**

```css
@import url("https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Bodoni+Moda:wght@400;900&display=swap");
@font-face { font-family: "Custom"; src: url("./fonts/custom.woff2") format("woff2"); }
.title { font: 900 240px/0.88 "Bodoni Moda", serif; letter-spacing: -0.04em; }
.subtitle { font: 200 18px "Inter", sans-serif; letter-spacing: 0.4em; text-transform: uppercase; }
```

System fonts work. WOFF / WOFF2 via `@font-face`. Google Fonts via
`@import`. Variable font axes work where Stylo understands the
named-instance form. `line-height`, `letter-spacing`, `word-spacing`,
`text-transform`, `text-align`, `font-feature-settings`, `font-variant`
all work.

**Color + gradients**

```css
.scrim { background: linear-gradient(180deg, transparent 0%, #000a 60%, #000 100%); }
.aurora { background: conic-gradient(from 90deg at 30% 70%, #f06, #f60, #06f, #f06); }
.spot   { background: radial-gradient(ellipse at 30% 30%, #fffa 0%, transparent 60%); }
```

Linear, radial, and conic gradients. Hex / rgb / rgba / hsl /
`color-mix()`. `currentColor` and CSS variables.

**Transforms**

```css
.tilt { transform: rotate(-3deg) scale(1.08) translateX(40px); transform-origin: top left; }
.skew { transform: skewX(-8deg); }
```

`translate`, `scale`, `rotate`, `skew`, `matrix()`, `transform-origin`,
3D transforms (`rotateX/Y/Z`, `perspective`).

**Borders, shadows, radius**

```css
.card { border: 2px solid #fff; border-radius: 24px; box-shadow: 0 24px 80px #000c, inset 0 0 0 1px #fff2; }
```

Multi-stop borders, `border-radius` (each corner independently),
`box-shadow` (multiple, including `inset`).

**clip-path** — *use this*

```css
.window { clip-path: circle(40% at 50% 50%); }
.notch  { clip-path: polygon(0 0, 100% 0, 100% 100%, 50% 70%, 0 100%); }
.tag    { clip-path: polygon(0 0, calc(100% - 24px) 0, 100% 50%, calc(100% - 24px) 100%, 0 100%); }
```

`circle()`, `polygon()`, and the box-keyword forms (`margin-box`,
`border-box`, `padding-box`, `content-box`) render today.
`inset()`, `ellipse()`, `path()`, and `url(#mask)` are silently dropped
— pick a polygon or circle approximation if you need a rect-with-radius
window.

**mix-blend-mode** — *use this*

```css
.title-diff   { color: #fff; mix-blend-mode: difference; }
.title-screen { mix-blend-mode: screen; }
.title-mult   { mix-blend-mode: multiply; }
```

All 16 CSS blend modes render through Vello's `peniko::Mix` —
`multiply, screen, overlay, darken, lighten, color-dodge, color-burn,
hard-light, soft-light, difference, exclusion, hue, saturation, color,
luminosity, normal`. `difference` over a generated shot is the canonical
"type carves through video" idiom and you should use it at least once
per spot.

**filter** — *use this*

```css
.soft     { filter: blur(8px); }
.poster   { filter: contrast(1.6) saturate(1.2); }
.mono     { filter: grayscale(1); }
.shifted  { filter: hue-rotate(180deg); }
.lifted   { filter: drop-shadow(0 12px 24px rgba(0,0,0,0.6)); }
.chained  { filter: blur(2px) saturate(1.3) brightness(1.1); }
```

`blur(Npx)`, `saturate(N)`, `brightness(N)`, `contrast(N)`,
`grayscale(N)`, `hue-rotate(Ndeg)`, `invert(N)`, `sepia(N)`, `opacity(N)`,
and `drop-shadow(X Y B color)` all render. Chains apply left-to-right
per CSS spec. Implemented as a render-to-image fallback in
`vendor/blitz-paint/src/render/filter.rs` — the element is painted into
a sidecar Vello scene, the resulting RGBA buffer is filtered via
`image::imageops::blur` for spatial blur + standard `feColorMatrix`
matrices for color filters, then composited back into the parent scene
as an image brush. The fallback expands the painted region by 3·sigma
for blur and by the offset + 3·sigma for drop-shadow, so soft halos
don't get clipped.

`backdrop-filter` does *not* render today (Stylo doesn't expose the
property under the Servo feature flag blitz-dom uses). Use a translucent
solid scrim or gradient scrim as a workaround.

**Standard CSS animations** — *use this*

```css
@keyframes title-in {
  0%   { transform: translateY(40px); opacity: 0; }
  60%  { transform: translateY(-4px); opacity: 1; }
  100% { transform: translateY(0);    opacity: 1; }
}
.title { animation: title-in 0.9s var(--ease-out-back) both; }

.subtitle { transition: opacity 0.4s ease-out 0.3s; opacity: 0; }
.scene.live .subtitle { opacity: 1; }
```

Stylo drives `@keyframes` and `transition` advance per-frame from the
scene clock — `render_offline.rs` resolves the document at every frame's
local time, so animations actually play in offline render. `linear`,
`ease`, `ease-in`, `ease-out`, `ease-in-out`, `cubic-bezier(…)`,
`steps(N [, jump-…])` all render. `animation-fill-mode`,
`animation-delay`, `animation-iteration-count`, `animation-direction`
work. Stagger by `:nth-child` + `animation-delay` math.

Springs / bounces / elastic / wiggle aren't representable as
cubic-bezier. For those, lay out the animation as a multi-stop
`@keyframes` timeline by hand — Stylo interpolates per-stop.

**Extended easing — paste from `eases.css`**

A sibling file ships the standard easings.net curves as CSS custom
properties — `var(--ease-out-back)`, `var(--ease-out-expo)`,
`var(--ease-out-quint)`, etc., 24 named eases total. Copy the `:root`
block from `vendor/workbooks/skills/gamut-director/eases.css` into each
scene's `<style>` (or `@import` it). Two scenes in every spot should
reach into the extended table — `ease-out` is fine but identical use of
plain `ease` across every cut is the AI-default tell.

```css
:root {
  --ease-out-back:  cubic-bezier(0.34, 1.56, 0.64, 1);
  --ease-out-quint: cubic-bezier(0.22, 1,    0.36, 1);
  --ease-out-expo:  cubic-bezier(0.16, 1,    0.3,  1);
  /* …see eases.css for the full set */
}

.title  { animation: enter 0.9s var(--ease-out-back) both; }
.kicker { animation: enter 1.4s var(--ease-out-quint) 0.2s both; }
```

**`<img>` element**

```html
<img src="./logo.svg" style="width: 120px; opacity: 0.9;">
<img src="./moodboard-1.png" style="position: absolute; inset: 0; object-fit: cover;">
```

Raster images (PNG / JPG / WebP) and SVG paint natively. Decoded via the
image crate's codec features in Blitz. `object-fit`, `object-position`
work.

### Variation across cuts — a hard rule

Within a single spot, **no two adjacent scenes may share the same
typographic treatment**. Same typeface across the spot is fine — that's
the through-line. Same size *and* position *and* motion is the
"AI-default" tell that flattens the work.

The good failure mode: "scene 1 uses Bodoni 240px center, scene 2 uses
Inter 22px upper-right, scene 3 is type-free, scene 4 uses JetBrains
Mono 18px corner tags." The bad failure mode: "scene 1 is Inter 88px
bottom-left, scene 2 is Inter 88px bottom-left, scene 3 is Inter 88px
bottom-left, scene 4 is Inter 88px bottom-left."

### Self-check before declaring a scene done

Ask yourself:

1. **Did I reuse the previous scene's lockup?** Same typeface + same
   size + same position + same motion. If yes — redo this one.
2. **Did I reach for `clip-path` or `mix-blend-mode` anywhere in the
   spot?** Neither one is mandatory in every scene, but a four-scene
   spot that doesn't touch either is using maybe 30% of the palette.
3. **Did I use anything from the extended ease table?** At least two
   scenes should be on `var(--ease-*)` curves, not plain `ease`.
4. **Did I let any scene have zero type?** Editorial silence is a real
   move. Captioning every cut isn't a requirement.
5. **Are my animations distinct per scene?** Four scenes all running the
   same `@keyframes slide-in` is the AI-default in motion form.

### Anti-pattern gallery — do NOT ship these

```css
/* ANTI-PATTERN 1: the AI-default lockup. Four scenes of this is failure. */
.title {
  position: absolute; left: 80px; bottom: 80px;
  font: 900 88px Inter, sans-serif;
  color: white;
  animation: fade-in 0.6s ease both;
}
```

```css
/* ANTI-PATTERN 2: same @keyframes recycled across every scene */
@keyframes enter { from { opacity: 0; transform: translateY(20px); } to { opacity: 1; transform: none; } }
.scene-1-title { animation: enter 0.6s ease both; }
.scene-2-title { animation: enter 0.6s ease both; }
.scene-3-title { animation: enter 0.6s ease both; }
.scene-4-title { animation: enter 0.6s ease both; }
```

```css
/* ANTI-PATTERN 3: everything centered, no negative-space awareness */
.scene { display: grid; place-items: center; }
.title { text-align: center; font-size: 120px; }
/* Repeated unmodified across all four scenes. */
```

```css
/* ANTI-PATTERN 4: plain `ease` everywhere, no extended curves used */
.a { animation: in 0.5s ease both; }
.b { animation: in 0.5s ease-in-out both; }
.c { animation: in 0.5s ease both; }
.d { animation: in 0.5s ease both; }
```

### Edge insets, contrast, reading rate — verify with the tools

- **Title-safe** is the center 80% (10% inset off each edge). Type
  across title-safe is a deliberate choice (brutalist, display-driven).
  Type across action-safe (center 90%, 5% inset) is a mistake on most
  platforms. `gamut::aspect::safe_areas(w, h)` returns the right
  rectangles per aspect.
- **Read the shot first.** `gamut image negative-space <png>` returns
  the eye-friendly grid cells with suggested text color + scrim opacity.
  Position type in the top-ranked zone unless you have a reason to
  fight the shot.
- **Contrast.** `gamut image contrast <png> --region X,Y,W,H
  --text-color #...` reports WCAG ratio + suggests a scrim if below
  threshold. WCAG AA is 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for 18pt+ or 14pt
  bold. Below AA is a deliberate choice (luxury whisper, brutalist
  clash), not an accident.
- **Reading rate** is ~2.5 chars/sec. Kinetic word-by-word reveals live
  in the 0.25–0.4s range; faster than 0.15s reads as a flicker.
- **Existing text in the shot.** `gamut image ocr <png>` (stubbed
  pending Fal got-ocr) detects baked-in text so you don't stack overlays
  on plate numbers or storefront signage.

## Step 6.5 — ten worked scene examples (study these)

Each example is a complete `<style>` + `<body>` block. They span the
palette — copy patterns from these directly into your scenes. They
deliberately don't share a lockup.

### Example 1 — Brutalist: Helvetica Black, all-caps, covers the frame

```html
<!doctype html>
<html><head><style>
  html, body { margin: 0; padding: 0; background: transparent; width: 100%; height: 100%; overflow: hidden; }
  body { font-family: "Helvetica Neue", Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif; color: #fff; }
  .smash {
    position: absolute; inset: 0;
    display: grid; grid-template-rows: 1fr auto;
    padding: 24px 32px;
  }
  .word {
    font-weight: 900;
    font-size: 26vw;
    line-height: 0.78;
    letter-spacing: -0.05em;
    text-transform: uppercase;
    margin: 0;
  }
  .word.two { text-align: right; transform: translateY(-0.05em); }
  .meta {
    font-size: 18px; letter-spacing: 0.3em; text-transform: uppercase;
    display: flex; justify-content: space-between;
  }
  @keyframes punch-in {
    0%   { transform: scale(1.04); opacity: 0; }
    60%  { transform: scale(1);    opacity: 1; }
    100% { transform: scale(1);    opacity: 1; }
  }
  .word { animation: punch-in 0.5s cubic-bezier(0.16, 1, 0.3, 1) both; }
  .word.two { animation-delay: 0.18s; }
</style></head>
<body>
  <div class="smash">
    <div>
      <h1 class="word">NOW</h1>
      <h1 class="word two">EVERYTHING</h1>
    </div>
    <div class="meta"><span>04 / SS26</span><span>FIELD NOTES</span></div>
  </div>
</body></html>
```

Design intent: type IS the image. Reads at any size, leans anti-pretty,
defies the safe-area convention deliberately.

### Example 2 — Editorial: Didone serif, asymmetric two-line moment

```html

### Example 6 — Typographic mask: clip-path carves a word-shape into the video

```html
<!doctype html>
<html><head><style>
  html, body { margin: 0; background: transparent; height: 100%; }
  .stage { position: absolute; inset: 0; display: grid; place-items: center; }
  /* Hex polygon that reads as a viewing port through the scene. */
  .port {
    width: 70vw; aspect-ratio: 16 / 7;
    background: #000;
    clip-path: polygon(8% 0, 92% 0, 100% 50%, 92% 100%, 8% 100%, 0% 50%);
    mix-blend-mode: lighten;
  }
  .legend {
    position: absolute; left: 8vw; bottom: 8vh;
    font: 800 22px "Helvetica Neue", sans-serif;
    color: #fff;
    letter-spacing: 0.18em; text-transform: uppercase;
  }
  @keyframes iris {
    from { clip-path: polygon(50% 50%, 50% 50%, 50% 50%, 50% 50%, 50% 50%, 50% 50%); }
    to   { clip-path: polygon(8% 0,    92% 0,    100% 50%, 92% 100%, 8% 100%, 0% 50%); }
  }
  .port { animation: iris 0.9s cubic-bezier(0.65, 0, 0.35, 1) both; }
</style></head>
<body>
  <div class="stage"><div class="port"></div></div>
  <div class="legend">Sector — 04 / observed</div>
</body></html>
```

Design intent: a polygon clip-path masks a black plate against the
moving footage; `mix-blend-mode: lighten` keeps the brightest pixels
of the video visible through the mask. The clip-path animates from a
collapsed point to its full hexagonal aperture — an iris reveal.
